title: Curry on Cotton
stainChemistry: Curry stains are dominated by turmeric pigment, intensified by other spice pigments and driven deep into cotton by the oil base (ghee, coconut oil, ground oil). Two rules matter more than the rest: (1) AVOID ALKALINE — turmeric pigment under alkaline conditions (ammonia, baking soda, soap scum) reversibly shifts to a much darker RED color. That red fades back to yellow under acid, but on cotton it can stain semi-permanently in the red form. (2) UV LIGHT FADES turmeric pigment — sunlight is a friend here, the opposite of the alkaline rule. Cotton itself tolerates IPA, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, sun. The challenge is the tenacious bond turmeric pigment forms with cellulose.
whyThisWorks: Oil-carrier first (NSD / Dawn) — turmeric pigment is protected from every subsequent chemistry while the oil is there. Then 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the color from the oxygen side, and sunlight drives the same fade from the UV side. Multiple cycles over a day or two are normal on curry — the stain is patient-removal, not power-removal. The alkaline-avoid rule is absolute: one ammonia-containing cleaner applied by accident can turn a yellow stain into a dark red one that's harder to remove.
defaultAssumption: Curry is one of the hardest home stains. Fresh + white cotton + no alkaline exposure = reasonable DIY but plan for 2–3 cycles. Aged + dark cotton = pro plateau likely. Anything that's seen ammonia or baking soda paste from a prior attempt = red-shifted, harder than it started.

spottingProtocol.technique: Remove bulk, then cold flush from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid curry off with a dull blade. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — heat drives the oil deeper. DO NOT use any alkaline cleaner (ammonia, baking soda, all-purpose spray) on curry; it shifts the yellow to red.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. The oil must come off first — turmeric pigment is protected from peroxide while oil is there. Rinse cool. Repeat if oil residue is still visible.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG on a clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: For aged / heavy curry where NSD alone doesn't clear the oil: POG tamp on a white cloth. Cotton tolerates POG. Ventilate.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂, sunny window or outdoor line
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply 3% H₂O₂ directly. Dwell 10 min wet, blot. Hang damp in direct sunlight — UV + peroxide oxidize turmeric pigment together. Repeat the peroxide + sun pass every 30–60 minutes until shadow fades.
Flush with NSD. No alkaline rinse — stays on the acid / neutral side throughout.
Launder hot per care label. Sun-dry when possible — any residual turmeric pigment continues to fade. Multiple sun cycles may be needed over 2 days on aged stains.

steps: 1. Scrape bulk. Cold flush from the back. No alkaline anywhere.
2. NSD — strip oil carrier first. Tamp, not scrub.
3. POG if oil is aged / heavy.
4. 3% H₂O₂ + 1–3 hours sunlight. Repeat every 30–60 min until fade.
5. NSD flush — stay on acid / neutral side throughout.
6. Hot wash + sun dry for residual fade.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry; ambient sun for fade
warnings: NEVER apply any alkaline cleaner — turmeric pigment red-shifts and becomes harder
Oil first — peroxide can't reach turmeric pigment through the oil carrier
Set honest expectations — aged curry may plateau at faint yellow even professionally

homeSolutions.agent: DON'T Use Baking Soda, Ammonia, or Alkaline Cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the single most important rule for curry on cotton. The yellow in curry comes from turmeric. If turmeric pigment touches ANYTHING alkaline — baking soda, ammonia, most all-purpose sprays, washing soda — it instantly shifts from yellow to dark RED, and that red form is much harder to remove. If you've already applied baking soda paste, soak the area in diluted white vinegar for 20 minutes to shift it back before anything else.

homeSolutions.agent: Scrape + Cold Water From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Scrape any solid curry off with a dull butter knife. Hold the BACK of the stained fabric under cold running water for a minute. Cold only — warm water drives the oil deeper into cotton.

Curry has oil (ghee, coconut, vegetable) as its carrier. That oil protects the yellow pigment from everything else until it's gone. Work Dawn dish soap directly into the stain with a soft toothbrush for 2–3 minutes, rinse cool. Repeat if you can still feel oiliness. You should see the stain lighten noticeably once the oil is out.

homeSolutions.agent: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% + Sunlight — the Real Fade
homeSolutions.instruction: This is where turmeric pigment actually fades. Pour 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the stain, let it bubble 10 min. Hang the damp garment in direct sunlight for an hour. Come back, re-apply peroxide, back in the sun, another hour. A full sunny afternoon of this often takes a curry stain from bright yellow to nearly gone. Aged curry can take 2–3 sunny days. Skip on dark cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia, chlorine bleach, or vinegar at the same time — one at a time with a rinse between. And never, ever put alkaline anything on a curry stain.

homeSolutions.instruction: Once the stain is faded, launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Line-dry in direct sunlight if possible — any residual turmeric pigment keeps fading under UV.

Curry is one of the hardest home stains. Fresh + caught fast on white cotton is winnable. Aged + dark cotton often plateaus at a faint yellow shadow — even professionals have limits here. If the stain has been through a hot dryer, iron, OR had alkaline cleaner applied, home chemistry may not fully clear it and honest escalation to a dry cleaner (with realistic expectations) is the call.

Take to a pro if: alkaline was applied (red-shift), the curry has been hot-dried, two peroxide + sun cycles haven't cleared it, or the cotton is dark dyed. Tell them: "Curry on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide + sunlight. [No alkaline used / baking soda was applied before I knew / hot-dried]."

steps: 1. NO alkaline cleaners on curry. Ever. Baking soda + curry = permanent red shift.
2. Scrape bulk, cold water flush from the BACK.
3. Dawn first — strip the oil carrier. 2–3 min, rinse cool.
4. 3% hydrogen peroxide + 1 hour direct sunlight. Repeat every 30–60 min. 2–3 cycles over a sunny day (or two days on aged stains).
5. Hot wash per care label. Line-dry in sun.
6. Escalate if alkaline was applied, hot-dried, two cycles plateau, or dark cotton.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if alkaline has been applied and turned yellow to red, if curry has been hot-dried, or if two peroxide + sun cycles plateau. Take to a dry cleaner and set realistic expectations — curry may leave a faint shadow.
warnings: ABSOLUTE: no alkaline cleaners on curry. Baking soda, ammonia, washing soda, all-purpose sprays = red permanent shift.
Oil first — peroxide can't work through the oil carrier
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Set honest expectations: aged curry plateaus are normal

neverDo: NEVER apply alkaline cleaners to curry — red-shifts the turmeric pigment
Never skip the oil-strip step — peroxide can't penetrate the carrier
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never promise full removal on aged curry — plateau is common
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, POG, 3% H₂O₂, UV sunlight, hot wash
Dyed cotton: peroxide + sun fades fabric dye too — test inside seam
Heat-durable fiber overall; the turmeric pigment is the limiting factor

materialWarnings: NO ALKALINE cleaners on curry — permanent red shift
Peroxide + sunlight is the patient fade workhorse

escalation.when: Alkaline cleaner was applied to curry (red-shift), curry heat-set by iron or dryer, two peroxide + sun cycles plateau, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Curry on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% H₂O₂ + sunlight. [No alkaline used / baking soda was applied before I knew / hot-dried.] Visible shadow: [yellow / red / faint]."

contextQuestions: Has any alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, all-purpose) been applied?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The alkaline-avoid rule is the curry-specific unlock. Normal stain advice says "baking soda paste" for everything — here it turns a yellow stain into a red one. Never on curry. Mustard-cotton shares this rule.
